stem:
A 29-year-old nulliparous woman presents to your general practice for the third time in 9 months distressed by severe irritability, low mood, anxiety, breast tenderness and bloating that begin around 7 days before menses and resolve fully within 2 days of menses starting. She has used the eCOPE prospective symptom diary for 3 consecutive cycles which shows at least 5 symptoms in the luteal phase with full resolution in the follicular phase and no symptoms outside the luteal phase.

On examination she is well, with a normal mood when reviewed in the follicular phase today. PHQ-9 outside the luteal phase is 2 of 27 and GAD-7 is 1 of 21; both rise sharply in the luteal phase. She has tried regular exercise and dietary change for 4 months without benefit. There is no history of bipolar affective disorder, no current alcohol or recreational drug use, no concomitant medications and no contraception is in use because she and her partner are happy with condoms.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 70/min, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Well, mood reactive; eCOPE diary: at least 5 luteal symptoms, full follicular resolution across 3 cycles
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 follicular 2/27 (rises sharply in luteal phase); GAD-7 follicular 1/21; TSH within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment?

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily commenced 14 days before expected menses each cycle
options.rationale: Luteal phase dosing of SSRI is supported by NICE and RANZCOG for premenstrual dysphoric disorder and works within hours rather than weeks; symptom improvement begins from the first treated cycle. (Source: RANZCOG Premenstrual Disorders; AMH; RANZCP)

options.text: Lithium carbonate 600 mg taken nightly with monthly serum lithium concentration monitoring
options.rationale: Lithium has no role in PMDD without bipolar disorder; it is a mood stabiliser for bipolar affective disorder and risks toxicity without indication. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g infusion during severe luteal flares in the day clinic
options.rationale: IV magnesium for PMDD flares is not standard care; oral magnesium has weak evidence but IV use lacks support and exposes the patient to procedural risk. (Source: RANZCOG Premenstrual Disorders)

options.text: Lamotrigine titrated over 6 weeks to 100 mg daily with rash monitoring during build up
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is used in bipolar depression maintenance; it is not first-line for PMDD and has rash risk that does not justify use here. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing drospirenone taken cyclically with a 4 day break
options.rationale: Drospirenone COCP is an option for PMDD but typically used continuously or with a 4-day break, with mixed evidence; for women not requiring contraception, luteal phase SSRI is preferred first line. (Source: RANZCOG Premenstrual Disorders)

explanation.summary: Premenstrual dysphoric disorder is the severe end of the premenstrual disorder spectrum, affecting around 3 to 8% of menstruating women. DSM-5 requires at least 5 symptoms confined to the luteal phase across 2 prospective cycles with full follicular phase remission and functional impairment. First-line pharmacotherapy is SSRI: either continuous, luteal-phase only (14 days before expected menses), or symptom onset. Luteal phase dosing is convenient and effective from the first cycle, unlike SSRIs in major depression. Combined oral contraceptive pills with drospirenone are an alternative for women also seeking contraception. Cognitive behaviour therapy and lifestyle measures (exercise, sleep, reduced caffeine) are adjuncts.
key_points: DSM-5 PMDD: at least 5 symptoms, luteal only, functional impairment, prospective diary
First-line: SSRI continuous or luteal phase only
Alternative: drospirenone COCP if contraception also wanted
Onset of SSRI benefit in PMDD is within hours, not weeks
explanation.pearls: Luteal phase dosing of sertraline 50 mg from cycle day 14 until day 1 of menses produces response from the first treated cycle in PMDD; this rapid action distinguishes PMDD treatment from SSRI use in major depressive disorder where weeks of dosing are needed.

stem:
A 30-year-old G2P2 woman presents to your general practice at 6 weeks postpartum for her routine review. She has had two uncomplicated vaginal deliveries; the youngest is exclusively breastfed and feeding 3 to 4 hourly day and night. Her previous postnatal contraception was a progesterone only pill that she found unreliable and she has a 12 year history of menorrhagia previously controlled on combined oral contraceptive pill. She and her partner have completed their family but are not yet committed to permanent sterilisation. She has had no bleeding for the last 7 days and there has been no resumption of menses.

On examination she is well. Body mass index is 25 kg/m2. Blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg. Speculum examination shows a clean cervix and bimanual examination reveals a normally involuted uterus, no adnexal tenderness and no mass. A urine pregnancy test in the consulting room is negative. There is no current pelvic infection and she has had no STI risk in the last 6 months.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 70/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 25; speculum clean cervix; bimanual: involuted uterus, no adnexal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Urine pregnancy test negative; no current STI symptoms; no high-risk sexual contact in last 6 months

lead_in: What is the most appropriate contraceptive recommendation today?

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill starting today and continuing without breaks
options.rationale: COCP is contraindicated in breastfeeding women under 6 weeks postpartum and cautioned until 6 months; it can reduce milk supply and is not the best option here. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception)

options.text: Insert a levonorgestrel releasing intrauterine system in the same consultation today
options.rationale: At 6 weeks postpartum after lochia has settled, LNG-IUS can be safely inserted; it provides effective contraception, treats her menorrhagia history, and is compatible with breastfeeding. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception; Family Planning Australia)

options.text: Insert a copper intrauterine device at 12 weeks postpartum after a repeat speculum examination
options.rationale: Copper IUD is an option but delays initiation by 6 weeks unnecessarily; it also worsens menstrual bleeding which is undesirable given her menorrhagia history. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception)

options.text: Defer all contraception until resumption of menses then start the progesterone only pill
options.rationale: Lactational amenorrhoea is not sufficiently reliable beyond 6 months or after introduction of solids; gaps in contraception in an actively partnered woman risks unplanned pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception)

options.text: Administer depot medroxyprogesterone 150 mg intramuscular injection today and repeat every 12 weeks
options.rationale: Depot is an option but is associated with weight gain and bone density loss with long use; the patient prefers a long acting reversible method that also treats her menorrhagia. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception)

explanation.summary: Long-acting reversible contraception is recommended as a first-line postpartum option for women not seeking immediate further pregnancy. LNG-IUS can be inserted at the time of caesarean section, within 48 hours of vaginal birth (immediate), or after 4 weeks postpartum (delayed); insertion between 48 hours and 4 weeks carries higher expulsion risk and is avoided. By 4 to 6 weeks lochia has typically settled and the uterus has involuted; the LNG-IUS is safe and effective in breastfeeding women and reduces menstrual blood loss substantially. The copper IUD is an alternative but increases bleeding, which is undesirable in women with prior menorrhagia. Combined hormonal methods are contraindicated under 6 weeks postpartum due to VTE risk and may impair lactation; progesterone only methods are compatible with breastfeeding throughout.
key_points: Postpartum LNG-IUS: at delivery, within 48 hours, or after 4 weeks
Avoid insertion 48 hours to 4 weeks (high expulsion)
LNG-IUS safe in breastfeeding and treats menorrhagia
COCP contraindicated under 6 weeks; progesterone only methods OK
explanation.pearls: Immediate postpartum LNG-IUS insertion within 48 hours of vaginal birth is now supported in Australian practice but carries an expulsion rate of approximately 15 to 20% at 6 months; delayed insertion at 4 to 6 weeks balances expulsion risk and ease of access per Family Planning Australia.

stem:
You are the obstetric registrar called to the high dependency obstetric area for a 36-year-old G2P1 woman at 35+2 weeks being managed for severe pre-eclampsia. Two hours ago she had a 4 g intravenous magnesium sulfate loading dose followed by a 1 g/hour maintenance infusion. Her blood pressure is now controlled on a labetalol infusion. She has had no seizure and the cardiotocograph remains reassuring.

The bedside midwife calls you because over the last 30 minutes the patient has become progressively drowsy, slurring her speech. Patellar reflexes are now absent bilaterally. Respiratory rate has fallen to 9 breaths per minute and she is not coughing on suctioning. Hourly urine output has dropped from 60 mL/hour to 15 mL/hour over the last 2 hours. The renal team reports her serum creatinine has risen from 78 to 156 micromol/L since admission. The point of care magnesium concentration just back from the laboratory is 4.5 mmol/L (therapeutic 2 to 3.5 mmol/L).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 9/min, SpO2 94% room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal heart 140/min
data_table.Examination: Drowsy with slurred speech; patellar reflexes absent; not coughing on suctioning; urine output 15 mL/hour
data_table.Investigations: Magnesium 4.5 mmol/L (therapeutic 2-3.5); creatinine 156 micromol/L (was 78); CTG: baseline 140/min, normal variability, no decelerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous naloxone 400 mcg titrated for respiratory depression and reassess every 5 minutes
options.rationale: Naloxone reverses opioid not magnesium toxicity; she has had no opioid and her respiratory depression is from supratherapeutic magnesium. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Intubate immediately and ventilate at FiO2 100% until magnesium clearance restores breathing
options.rationale: Intubation and ventilation are supportive but calcium gluconate is the direct antidote and should be given first; intubation is only required if calcium plus airway support fails to restore respiratory drive. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Cease the magnesium infusion and administer intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 3 minutes
options.rationale: Magnesium toxicity in pre-eclampsia is reversed with intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g (10 mL of 10% solution) given over 3 minutes plus immediate cessation of the magnesium infusion; this antagonises magnesium at the neuromuscular junction. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy; RANZCOG Hypertensive Disorders)

options.text: Switch antihypertensive control to oral nifedipine 20 mg and continue the magnesium infusion
options.rationale: Switching antihypertensives does not reverse magnesium toxicity, and continued magnesium with falling renal clearance will worsen the neuromuscular and respiratory depression. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg to accelerate magnesium clearance and a fluid bolus of 250 mL saline
options.rationale: Frusemide accelerates magnesium clearance but the priority is immediate antagonism with calcium; a fluid bolus in the context of pre-eclampsia and oliguria risks pulmonary oedema. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Magnesium sulfate halves eclampsia risk and is standard for severe pre-eclampsia. Toxicity is dose dependent and renal clearance dependent. Therapeutic concentration is 2 to 3.5 mmol/L; loss of patellar reflexes appears around 3.5 to 5 mmol/L, respiratory depression around 5 to 6.5 mmol/L, and cardiac arrest above 6.5 mmol/L. Oliguria and rising creatinine cut magnesium clearance, so urine output and renal function must be monitored. Treatment of toxicity is immediate cessation of the magnesium infusion and intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 3 minutes; supportive ventilation is added if respiratory depression persists. Magnesium concentration should be checked 6-hourly in renal impairment and the infusion rate halved when creatinine clearance falls.
key_points: Therapeutic Mg 2 to 3.5 mmol/L; toxicity from 3.5 upwards
Loss of reflexes first, then respiratory depression, then arrest
Antidote: calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 3 minutes
Renal impairment cuts clearance; reduce or stop infusion
explanation.pearls: In severe pre-eclampsia, hourly patellar reflexes, respiratory rate and urine output are the bedside markers of magnesium safety; a fall in urine output below 30 mL/hour over 4 hours mandates magnesium concentration check and infusion rate review per SOMANZ guidance.

stem:
A 33-year-old G1P0 woman presents to the early pregnancy assessment service at 7 weeks gestation after a fresh embryo transfer through her local IVF clinic. She has had right iliac fossa pain over the last 24 hours, described as constant and rated 4 of 10. There has been a small amount of pink spotting today. There is no shoulder tip pain, no syncope and no significant per vaginal bleeding.

On examination she is haemodynamically stable. Abdomen is soft with mild right iliac fossa tenderness, no rebound and no guarding. Speculum examination shows a closed cervix with a small amount of fresh blood. Bimanual examination reveals fullness in the right adnexa. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an intrauterine gestational sac at 7 weeks with a fetal pole, crown rump length 10 mm and fetal cardiac activity present; a separate 3 cm complex right adnexal mass adjacent to the right ovary and a small amount of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Serum beta-hCG is 14,200 IU/L; haemoglobin 124 g/L; her blood group is A positive.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 86/min, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, mild right iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism; speculum closed cervix small fresh blood; bimanual right adnexal fullness
data_table.Investigations: TV US: viable 7-week intrauterine pregnancy with fetal cardiac activity; 3 cm complex right adnexal mass; small POD fluid; beta-hCG 14,200 IU/L; Hb 124 g/L; group A positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Suction curettage of the uterine cavity followed by methotrexate for the adnexal pregnancy
options.rationale: Suction curettage destroys a viable intrauterine pregnancy that this patient wishes to continue; it is not the standard management for heterotopic pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Single dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 with weekly beta-hCG monitoring thereafter
options.rationale: Methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated when a concurrent viable intrauterine pregnancy is present; it cannot be used in heterotopic pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG and ultrasound surveillance over the next 7 days
options.rationale: An adnexal mass over 3.5 cm or with significant pain warrants surgical management; expectant management cannot be safely offered with active symptoms and a viable intrauterine pregnancy to protect. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube while preserving the intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: In heterotopic pregnancy with a symptomatic adnexal mass, surgical removal (typically laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube) is recommended; the intrauterine pregnancy is preserved by careful technique avoiding uterine manipulation. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin 625 IU and discharge with early pregnancy unit review in 48 hours
options.rationale: She is rhesus positive so anti-D is not indicated; discharge with symptomatic heterotopic pregnancy risks tubal rupture and miscarriage of the intrauterine pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Anti-D Immunoglobulin)

explanation.summary: Heterotopic pregnancy (concurrent intrauterine and ectopic pregnancy) is rare in spontaneous conception (1 in 30,000) but occurs in approximately 1 in 100 IVF pregnancies. Diagnosis is missed when an early intrauterine pregnancy is found and the adnexae are not carefully scanned. Beta-hCG is unreliable for diagnosis because the intrauterine pregnancy produces normally rising hCG. Methotrexate is contraindicated because of teratogenicity to the intrauterine pregnancy. Management is surgical: laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube while minimising uterine manipulation. Reported intrauterine pregnancy survival after surgical management of heterotopic pregnancy is approximately 60 to 70%.
key_points: Heterotopic risk approximately 1 in 100 in IVF pregnancies
Always scan adnexae even if intrauterine pregnancy is seen after IVF
Methotrexate contraindicated due to teratogenicity to intrauterine pregnancy
Surgical: laparoscopic salpingectomy with minimal uterine manipulation
explanation.pearls: Among IVF pregnancies, approximately 1 in 100 are heterotopic versus 1 in 30,000 of spontaneously conceived pregnancies; in any IVF pregnancy with adnexal pain the contralateral tube must be carefully assessed despite the comfort of a confirmed intrauterine sac.

stem:
A 35-year-old woman is referred to your colposcopy clinic with a positive cervical screening result. Eighteen months ago she had a large loop excision of the transformation zone for histologically confirmed HSIL (CIN 3) with clear excision margins of 4 mm. She had a routine test of cure cervical screening at 12 months which was reported as HPV oncogenic types not detected and she was advised to return to the routine 5 yearly screening cycle. She presented for an opportunistic test 6 months early following a recent change of general practitioner.

The current result reports HPV oncogenic type 16 detected, with reflex liquid based cytology showing HSIL. She is asymptomatic, with no intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding and no abnormal discharge. She is in a stable monogamous relationship and uses condoms intermittently. Her general health is good and her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago. On examination the cervix appears macroscopically healthy with no visible lesion; bimanual is unremarkable. Pelvic ultrasound has been arranged but is not yet completed.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Cervix macroscopically healthy with no visible lesion; bimanual unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: HPV oncogenic type 16 detected; reflex cytology HSIL; prior LLETZ for CIN 3 with 4 mm clear margins; 12-month test of cure HPV not detected

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed to total abdominal hysterectomy without prior colposcopy or histology confirmation
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is not justified before histological confirmation and the patient deserves the option of fertility-sparing excisional treatment. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat HPV based cervical screening in 12 months without colposcopy or biopsy intervention
options.rationale: HSIL cytology with HPV type 16 cannot be observed; the recurrence risk after prior CIN 3 is too high to defer histological assessment. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Order pelvic magnetic resonance imaging to stage suspected invasive cervical malignancy first
options.rationale: There is no clinical evidence of invasive disease; staging MRI is appropriate only after histological confirmation of invasion, not before colposcopy. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat cone biopsy by cold knife conisation in the operating theatre under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Cold knife conisation is reserved for cases where colposcopy cannot adequately visualise the lesion or where adenocarcinoma in situ is suspected; LLETZ remains preferred for repeat squamous excision. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Colposcopy with directed biopsy and consideration of repeat excisional treatment if confirmed
options.rationale: HPV type 16 with HSIL cytology after prior CIN 3 treatment requires colposcopy with biopsy; further excisional treatment is offered if recurrence is confirmed because residual or recurrent CIN occurs in approximately 5 to 10% even with clear margins. (Source: NCSP Guidelines; RANZCOG Cervical Screening)

explanation.summary: Following excisional treatment for high grade squamous intraepithelial lesion, the Australian National Cervical Screening Program recommends a test of cure (co-test HPV plus liquid based cytology) at 12 months. A negative test of cure returns the woman to routine 5 yearly screening. A positive HPV result or any cytological abnormality at test of cure requires colposcopy. Recurrent or persistent CIN affects approximately 5 to 10% of women after LLETZ even with clear margins because HPV is a field disease and reinfection or persistence at the new transformation zone can occur. HPV type 16 carries the highest risk of invasive progression. Management of recurrence is repeat colposcopy with biopsy and repeat excisional treatment if confirmed; hysterectomy is reserved for older women who have completed family or where excision is technically not feasible.
key_points: Australian test of cure: HPV + cytology at 12 months post LLETZ
Any HPV positive or cytology abnormal: colposcopy
5 to 10% recurrence even with clear margins
HPV type 16: highest invasive progression risk
explanation.pearls: HPV type 16 carries approximately twice the absolute progression risk of other oncogenic HPV types per Australian NCSP modelling; positivity at test of cure mandates colposcopy regardless of cytology result.

stem:
A 22-year-old G1P0 woman had suction evacuation 4 weeks ago for a complete hydatidiform molar pregnancy diagnosed at 9 weeks gestation by uterine size larger than dates, theca lutein cysts, and 'snowstorm' ultrasound. Histology confirmed a complete mole with no evidence of invasive trophoblast on initial review. She is being followed in the Australian gestational trophoblastic registry through your tertiary unit.

Her pre-evacuation beta-hCG was 250,000 IU/L. Weekly serial concentrations have been 18,000 on day 7, 12,000 on day 14, 13,000 on day 21 and 14,000 today on day 28. She is otherwise well, with no abdominal pain, no abnormal bleeding and no respiratory or neurological symptoms. Her chest radiograph is clear without nodules. Pelvic ultrasound today shows a normally involuted uterus, no residual molar tissue and normal adnexae. Liver and renal function are within normal limits. She and her partner use barrier contraception.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Well; uterus involuted; no abnormal bleeding; chest clear; no neurological deficits
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG day 7: 18,000; day 14: 12,000; day 21: 13,000; day 28: 14,000 IU/L; CXR clear; pelvic US no residual molar tissue; LFTs and renal within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Single agent methotrexate chemotherapy 50 mg intramuscular on day 1, 3, 5, 7 every 2 weeks
options.rationale: The patient meets FIGO criteria for post-molar GTN (beta-hCG plateau over 3 weeks of 4 values within plus or minus 10%); with a FIGO low-risk score, single agent methotrexate is the standard initial chemotherapy. (Source: RANZCOG GTN; FIGO 2018)

options.text: Combination chemotherapy with EMA-CO protocol (etoposide, methotrexate, dactinomycin, vincristine)
options.rationale: EMA-CO is reserved for high-risk GTN (WHO/FIGO prognostic score 7 or higher) or methotrexate-resistant disease; her low-risk score puts her in the single agent group first. (Source: RANZCOG GTN; FIGO 2018)

options.text: Repeat suction curettage of the uterine cavity for presumed retained molar tissue under anaesthesia
options.rationale: There is no ultrasound evidence of residual molar tissue and repeat evacuation does not improve outcome for GTN; chemotherapy is the recommended treatment for the rising or plateau beta-hCG. (Source: RANZCOG GTN; FIGO 2018)

options.text: Continue weekly beta-hCG monitoring without any intervention until 6 months post evacuation
options.rationale: Plateau over 3 weeks meets FIGO criteria for GTN diagnosis; further surveillance without treatment risks progression to choriocarcinoma. (Source: RANZCOG GTN; FIGO 2018)

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy with preservation of both ovaries as the definitive treatment
options.rationale: Hysterectomy may be offered for low-risk GTN in women who have completed family but is not first-line in a nulliparous 22-year-old; chemotherapy preserves fertility with equivalent cure rates. (Source: RANZCOG GTN)

explanation.summary: Following evacuation of a complete hydatidiform mole, beta-hCG surveillance detects gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN) which develops in approximately 15 to 20% of complete moles and 1 to 5% of partial moles. FIGO criteria for GTN are (1) plateau of 4 beta-hCG values over 3 weeks within plus or minus 10%, (2) rise of 10% or more over 3 consecutive weekly values, (3) persistent detectable beta-hCG at 6 months post evacuation, or (4) histological choriocarcinoma. Once GTN is diagnosed, staging (FIGO 2018) plus the WHO prognostic score determines low risk (score under 7) versus high risk treatment. Single agent methotrexate (with folinic acid rescue) is first-line for low risk disease; high risk disease requires EMA-CO. Cure rates approach 100% in low risk and 80 to 90% in high risk GTN; subsequent fertility is preserved.
key_points: FIGO post-molar GTN criteria: plateau, rise, persistent at 6 months, or histology
Score under 7 = low risk: single agent methotrexate
Score 7 or higher = high risk: EMA-CO combination
Cure rates approach 100% low risk, 80 to 90% high risk, fertility preserved
explanation.pearls: After successful chemotherapy for low-risk gestational trophoblastic neoplasia in Australia, contraception is recommended for at least 12 months and pregnancy avoided to ensure beta-hCG surveillance is interpretable and to allow detection of late recurrence.

stem:
A 30-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder presents to your psychiatry clinic for pre-conception counselling. She has been stable on lithium 800 mg nocte for 5 years with a serum concentration consistently 0.8 mmol/L; recent renal function and TSH are normal. She had three depressive recurrences and two manic episodes before lithium was started, with two psychiatric admissions during her teens. She has been well for the last 2 years and is in a stable partnership. She and her partner are now planning to conceive within 6 months.

She has read online that lithium is teratogenic and is considering stopping it before pregnancy. She has not had a recent mood relapse and her last quetiapine prescription was 4 years ago. She has no history of alcohol or substance use, body mass index is 24 kg/m2, blood pressure 118/70, and she is taking folate 5 mg daily. She has no thyroid or renal disease, no cardiac history, and her parents and siblings have no congenital heart disease.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 118/70 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mood euthymic, no psychomotor change; insight preserved; BMI 24
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.8 mmol/L; creatinine 68 micromol/L; TSH 1.6 mIU/L; calcium within normal limits
data_table.Medications: Lithium 800 mg nocte for 5 years; folate 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Stop lithium during first trimester and resume in second trimester to avoid cardiac teratogenicity
options.rationale: Interrupted dosing increases relapse risk and the absolute Ebstein anomaly risk on lithium is small (around 1 in 1000); continuous prophylaxis is preferred in high recurrence risk women. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health)

options.text: Continue lithium with frequent serum monitoring and detailed fetal cardiac ultrasound at 20 weeks
options.rationale: For high recurrence risk bipolar disorder, continuation of lithium across pregnancy with serum monitoring (more frequent in third trimester) and 18 to 20 week fetal echocardiography is preferred to discontinuation; the absolute risk of Ebstein anomaly is only about 1 in 1000. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health; SOMANZ)

options.text: Switch from lithium to sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily prior to conception for tighter mood stability
options.rationale: Valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential because of high teratogenicity (neural tube defects, dysmorphic features, neurodevelopmental impairment); this is the wrong move. (Source: AMH; RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health)

options.text: Switch to lamotrigine 200 mg daily prior to conception with slow titration and rash monitoring
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is preferred for bipolar depression maintenance rather than mania prevention; a stable patient on effective lithium should not switch without strong indication. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health)

options.text: Cease lithium prior to conception and manage prophylactically with no medication during pregnancy
options.rationale: Discontinuation of lithium is associated with approximately 50% relapse risk in pregnancy and postpartum; unmedicated bipolar pregnancy in a woman with prior admissions exposes mother and foetus to greater risk than continued lithium. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health; SafeScript)

explanation.summary: Bipolar disorder in pregnancy carries high recurrence risk if mood stabiliser is stopped, particularly in women with multiple prior episodes. Lithium continuation in pregnancy is supported by RANZCP for high relapse risk women: the absolute risk of Ebstein anomaly is approximately 1 to 2 per 1000 (relative risk doubled but small absolute increase) and is detectable on fetal echocardiography at 18 to 20 weeks. Lithium clearance rises in pregnancy and falls sharply at delivery so dose adjustment and frequent serum monitoring (monthly first and second trimester, fortnightly third) are essential. Valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential. The postpartum period carries up to 50% relapse risk in bipolar disorder and lithium prophylaxis around delivery is highly protective.
key_points: Discontinuation: approximately 50% relapse risk pregnancy and postpartum
Lithium Ebstein anomaly absolute risk approximately 1 in 1000
Fetal echocardiography at 18 to 20 weeks
Valproate contraindicated in women of childbearing potential
explanation.pearls: Postpartum lithium relapse prophylaxis is highly protective for bipolar disorder; the postpartum period has approximately 50% mood recurrence risk untreated in bipolar disorder per RANZCP perinatal guidance.

stem:
A 24-year-old man was started on olanzapine 10 mg nocte 4 weeks ago for a first episode of psychosis following review at your early psychosis intervention service. He is now responding well; positive symptoms have reduced and he has returned to part-time work. He has gained 3 kg over the last month, has a normal appetite and reports no sedation that he notices. He continues to live with his parents.

On review his blood pressure is 128/82 mmHg, heart rate 76, body mass index 27 kg/m2. There is no extrapyramidal sign, no akathisia, and his insight is intact. He has not used cannabis or alcohol since starting treatment, smokes 5 cigarettes a day with intent to quit, and has not started any other medication. There is no family history of diabetes or cardiovascular disease. He asks what investigations should be done to look after his physical health while on the medication. His baseline fasting glucose, lipids, prolactin and ECG before starting olanzapine were all within normal limits.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 128/82 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: BMI 27; no extrapyramidal signs, no akathisia; insight intact
data_table.Investigations: Baseline fasting glucose, lipids, prolactin and ECG within normal limits 4 weeks ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate physical health monitoring plan?

options.text: Repeat baseline lipids and fasting glucose only if metabolic symptoms develop in the next 12 months
options.rationale: Reactive monitoring misses asymptomatic metabolic syndrome which is the major cause of premature death in schizophrenia; routine monitoring schedule is required. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Fasting glucose and lipids monthly for the first 6 months then every 3 months indefinitely
options.rationale: Monthly fasting bloods are unnecessarily intensive and reduce adherence; the RANZCP standard is 12 weeks then yearly. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

options.text: Weight at every clinic visit; fasting lipids and glucose at 12 weeks then at 12 months and yearly
options.rationale: The RANZCP early psychosis monitoring schedule is weight at every visit, BMI plus waist circumference, fasting lipids and glucose at 12 weeks then 12 months then yearly while on antipsychotic; this catches early metabolic syndrome which is the main cardiovascular risk on antipsychotics. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Haemoglobin A1c only at 6 months as the single marker of glucose intolerance during treatment
options.rationale: HbA1c can miss rapid onset olanzapine-induced diabetes; the RANZCP standard uses fasting glucose because of the speed of glycaemic change on olanzapine. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test at 3 months and again at 12 months as the gold standard test
options.rationale: OGTT is reserved for borderline fasting glucose; routine OGTT screening on antipsychotic is not the recommended schedule. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic-associated cardiometabolic disease is the leading cause of premature mortality in schizophrenia. Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic risk; aripiprazole, ziprasidone and lurasidone carry the lowest. The RANZCP and eTG-Psychotropic standard schedule is: weight at every visit; baseline plus 12 weeks plus 12 months then yearly fasting lipids and glucose; ECG and prolactin at baseline plus annually; QTc check after dose change on QT-prolonging drugs. Switching antipsychotic to a metabolically safer agent should be considered when weight gain exceeds 5 to 7% of baseline, fasting glucose rises above 5.6 mmol/L, or lipids fall outside target. Smoking cessation, exercise and dietetic input are part of the package.
key_points: Standard: weight at every visit; fasting lipids and glucose 12 weeks then yearly
Plus ECG and prolactin baseline and yearly
Switch to metabolically safer agent if weight gain over 5 to 7%
Olanzapine and clozapine highest metabolic risk; aripiprazole lowest
explanation.pearls: Olanzapine causes the largest mean weight gain (around 4 kg over 12 weeks) and the largest glucose change of standard antipsychotics; switching to aripiprazole 15 mg or lurasidone 40 mg is the most metabolically favourable single-step option per RANZCP.

stem:
A 52-year-old man is admitted to the emergency department by his wife with confusion since this morning, three days after stopping all alcohol prior to a planned elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He has been drinking 1 litre of spirits daily for 15 years with previous detoxification attempts that he did not complete. He has not eaten well for 2 weeks because of biliary pain. He has had two prior unwitnessed seizures that he attributes to alcohol withdrawal.

On examination he is disoriented to time and place with visual hallucinations of small insects on the wall. He has coarse tremor of the outstretched hands, diaphoresis and gait ataxia when assisted to stand. There is horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze in both directions. He is afebrile and there is no focal neurology. Capillary glucose is 4.8 mmol/L. The on call medical registrar has just confirmed sodium 138, potassium 3.4, magnesium 0.62 mmol/L and an ethanol concentration of 0 mmol/L. The bedside CIWA score is 24.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 178/95 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 37.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Disoriented, visual hallucinations, coarse tremor, diaphoresis, gait ataxia, horizontal nystagmus on lateral gaze; CIWA 24
data_table.Investigations: Capillary glucose 4.8 mmol/L; sodium 138, potassium 3.4, magnesium 0.62 mmol/L; ethanol concentration 0 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Mechanical restraint with observation while observing for self-resolution within 12 hours
options.rationale: Restraint without pharmacotherapy is unsafe and unethical; delirium tremens has a mortality up to 15% if untreated, and Wernicke encephalopathy is preventable with timely thiamine. (Source: NHMRC Alcohol Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous haloperidol 5 mg with maintenance of glucose 5% infusion plus oral thiamine 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are contraindicated in alcohol withdrawal because they lower seizure threshold; glucose without prior parenteral thiamine precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy in deficient patients. (Source: NHMRC Alcohol Guidelines; eTG: Substance Use)

options.text: Oral diazepam 20 mg every hour titrated to a CIWA score under 10 with oral thiamine 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Oral dosing in delirium tremens with CIWA 24 has unreliable absorption and is too slow; suspected Wernicke needs parenteral high-dose thiamine, not oral. (Source: NHMRC Alcohol Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous thiamine 300 mg three times daily for 3 days plus diazepam 10 mg IV with magnesium replacement
options.rationale: Wernicke encephalopathy is suggested by the triad of confusion, ataxia and ophthalmoplegia in an alcohol-dependent patient; treatment is parenteral thiamine 300 mg three times daily for 3 days (Australian high-dose Pabrinex equivalent) given before any glucose load, plus benzodiazepine for delirium tremens and magnesium replacement. (Source: NHMRC Alcohol Guidelines; eTG: Substance Use; AMH)

options.text: Intravenous chlormethiazole 320 mg three times daily plus oral thiamine 100 mg daily with magnesium
options.rationale: Chlormethiazole is rarely used in Australia due to respiratory depression and dependence risk; diazepam or lorazepam are preferred. Oral thiamine is inadequate in suspected Wernicke. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Delirium tremens is the most severe alcohol withdrawal syndrome, typically peaking 48 to 96 hours after the last drink, with mortality up to 15% if untreated. Wernicke encephalopathy is a separate but co-occurring thiamine deficiency syndrome of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia; only one feature is required for clinical diagnosis. The Australian standard is parenteral thiamine 300 mg three times daily for 3 days before any glucose-containing fluid because glucose precipitates Wernicke in deficient patients. Delirium tremens is treated with intravenous diazepam (Australian preference) titrated to CIWA, with magnesium replacement, prevention of falls, and treatment of co-existing infection. Antipsychotics are avoided because they lower seizure threshold.
key_points: Wernicke triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (one feature enough)
Parenteral thiamine 300 mg three times daily for 3 days, before glucose
DT: diazepam titrated to CIWA, plus magnesium and infection workup
Avoid antipsychotics in withdrawal: lower seizure threshold
explanation.pearls: Untreated Wernicke encephalopathy progresses to Korsakoff dementia in approximately 80% within weeks; only one of the triad features is required to mandate parenteral thiamine 300 mg three times daily per AMH and eTG-Substance Use.

stem:
A 35-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine 400 mg daily for 6 months through your community early psychosis service. He has had a good clinical response with reduction in auditory hallucinations and stable mood. He attends today for his routine 4-weekly full blood count as required by the Australian Clozapine Patient Monitoring Service.

He reports no fever, sore throat, mouth ulcers, or other infective symptoms; he denies any recent travel and remains a non smoker. His weight has been stable; he is up to date with vaccinations and lives with supportive parents. There has been no change in medication apart from a recent course of amoxicillin clavulanate for an upper respiratory tract infection 3 weeks ago. Today's full blood count comes back from the laboratory while he is still in the waiting area: white cell count 3.4 x10^9/L, absolute neutrophil count 1.2 x10^9/L (1.5-7.0), haemoglobin 142 g/L, platelets 198 x10^9/L. His previous monthly counts have all been within the green range.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Asymptomatic; no fever, sore throat or mouth ulcer; no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: WCC 3.4 x10^9/L; ANC 1.2 x10^9/L (1.5-7.0); Hb 142 g/L; platelets 198 x10^9/L; prior monthly counts all within green range
data_table.Medications: Clozapine 400 mg daily for 6 months; recent amoxicillin clavulanate course completed 3 weeks ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Add lithium 600 mg nightly to stimulate neutrophil production and continue current clozapine dose
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation of neutrophil count is not a recognised CPMS response and cannot be used to bypass the cessation rule; clinical safety requires stopping clozapine. (Source: CPMS Australia)

options.text: Continue clozapine at the same dose and recheck the full blood count in 7 days as outpatient
options.rationale: ANC under 1.5 x10^9/L is the Australian CPMS red zone for which clozapine must be ceased; continuing risks agranulocytosis. (Source: CPMS Australia; AMH)

options.text: Subcutaneous filgrastim 5 mcg/kg daily to support neutrophil count and continue clozapine therapy
options.rationale: Filgrastim is reserved for confirmed agranulocytosis under haematology supervision; it is not used to keep a patient on clozapine in the red zone. (Source: HSANZ Position Statement)

options.text: Reduce the clozapine dose by 50% and recheck the full blood count in 48 hours
options.rationale: There is no dose reduction step in the Australian CPMS protocol; the response to a red ANC is mandatory cessation and daily monitoring. (Source: CPMS Australia)

options.text: Cease clozapine immediately, recheck full blood count every 48 hours, and notify CPMS today
options.rationale: An ANC under 1.5 x10^9/L is the CPMS red zone; mandatory cessation of clozapine with daily monitoring until counts recover above 2.0 x10^9/L and registry notification within 24 hours protects against fatal agranulocytosis. (Source: CPMS Australia; AMH)

explanation.summary: Clozapine is the only TGA-approved antipsychotic for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and produces neutropenia in approximately 3% and agranulocytosis in approximately 0.8% of patients, with highest risk in the first 18 weeks. Australian patients are mandatorily registered with the Clozapine Patient Monitoring Service (CPMS) for full blood count surveillance: weekly for 18 weeks, then 4 weekly. Result categories are green (ANC over 2.0), amber (1.5 to 2.0, twice weekly FBC) and red (ANC under 1.5, mandatory cessation). Cessation is permanent unless a haematologist confirms a benign ethnic neutropenia variant with separate CPMS rules. Suspected agranulocytosis with infection symptoms requires hospital admission, reverse barrier nursing and treatment with broad spectrum antibiotics with consideration of G-CSF.
key_points: CPMS thresholds: green over 2.0; amber 1.5-2.0; red under 1.5 x10^9/L
Red zone: mandatory cessation, daily FBC, CPMS notification
Highest risk window: first 18 weeks
Resumption requires haematologist confirmation of benign variant
explanation.pearls: Benign ethnic neutropenia is recognised in CPMS Australia and may allow clozapine continuation with separate thresholds (ANC under 1.0 cessation rather than 1.5), but requires haematologist confirmation of the variant rather than informal assumption based on ethnicity.
